transcript.text: This meeting is being recorded.
Hey, how are you?
I'm well, how are you?
Nice to congratulate you on, see.
Aw, thank you.
Good to finally chat again, you know?
Yeah, are you in the new office?
I am, I am in the new office.
This is the conference room, but there's only two rooms with tables right now.
We don't have desks yet.
They're getting delivered Friday.
That's exciting.
And then I needed to come here because they're receiving, we're receiving some deliveries today and nobody else is here.
And so then our nanny is with our daughter, getting her to nap somewhere.
And, you know, that's exciting.
Yeah.
How you been?
Great.
Great.
It feels like it's been, you know, on a rocket ship since we last talked, which was in 2022 early.
Thank you.
For both of us, it seems that way.
I pivoted from Amazon, which was much more application layered, back down to kind of what I know for the most part.
But I realized that I've changed in terms of what I enjoy doing and also my way of working.
Yeah.
Let's just say that the value I bring is really the top to bottom, full stack knowledge, which is pretty rare.
Also, it's kind of hard to bring that in an area that is very focused on a single layer of the stack, right?
Exactly.
Like right now, Microsoft is very hard to focus for me, which is fine.
And even though they said that, you know, we'd like you to come in and bring in the insights from the top layer so we know what to build at the hardware layer, right?
Because that's my.
It's still, you know, it's hard to change the way people have been working for years.

So, yeah, so all that to say, the reason I didn't want to talk to you was first, you know, I want to stay in touch because I work in an area that while it's the less commoditized of them all, because it's hard to get, right, to actually take a piece of the market from NVIDIA.
It's also slow.
And I'm looking for ways for me to maintain the muscle tone of the application layer, solution layer, those types of things.
Yeah.
So that's, you know, and also I think in the ocean of artificial intelligence where everyone labels themselves as an AI expert, you know, and also, know,
I have days when I doubt myself, even though I actually have proof of building something, not just playing with chat GPT.
But I'm looking to actually build human relationships, which is the complete opposite, right, of what most people are doing.
But I'm finding that that is the one thing that is going to not just help me survive through the hype cycle, but thrive.
So that's my motive.
I love that.
I guess maybe I can kind of catch you up on some things that are maybe not as apparent, like from the outside end, right?
So I don't know if I mentioned week closer, series A, we're announcing, I think, next week.
And...
I think part of that was, so we went through this journey of, hey, I was using AI to build workflows and to be able to publish content, which is something that I've always done for every company.
that's the reason I've been successful in my career.
But it was always hard because trying to explain a first principles thinking to editors and writers and then force every writer to write a certain way or research a certain way or plan.
Their articles a certain way was always hard.
so anyways, I did that.
DeepGram grew.
And then as I was doing those workshops, a lot of people were like, can you help customize workflows for me, but also help me with the strategy and also hire some people to help check the work.
I just don't have time to do it.
then we started saying yes to it.

But then I think pretty quickly as we started to scale that and we were at about like 10 clients, a little over a million in ARR last year, it was really clear one thing, like all these low-code tools just don't scale and don't work, right?
And they seem like the wrong form factor.
So if you think about Cursor or Replit or Lovable or Bolt's not near, they're a customer now, but it's just like a different form factor.
But especially Cursor, where engineers are way more productive now, right?
I don't know if you've played with Cursor at all, but our CTO, all our engineers are like, I couldn't live without Cursor anymore, right?
And it's just like giving them a superpower.

Then you look into it, you know, like how most knowledge workers, but especially the experts are using AI, they're either going and investing in just an obscene amount of time on low-code tools, aka like NAN and like workflow automation tools, AI workflows and things like that.
And just tinkering and tinkering and tinkering and trying to learn how to do prompting better and how to like stitch everything together, how to do retrieval, like just like the basic stuff, right?
And they're never going to be at the forefront of...
All those concepts and all those things.
And it's distracting them from actually delivering the output that they're trying to solve to begin with.
And as we went on that journey, it was just like a more accelerated journey because we weren't doing it for one company.
We were doing it for 10 and then 20.
And then we were quickly like, this is not going to work.
Right.
So essentially what we build under the hood is a coding agent that writes essentially workflows, AI workflows in code and represents them in code.
So think of like a form config, right, file, but for knowledge work.
So it's representing like the knowledge work itself.
Right.
And I can show you some stuff too.
But like essentially like all of these workflows that we build are all represented in code.
And And so like, let's say a deep researcher, right?
Like it goes, it does a bunch of stuff.
It parallel executes research questions, you know, and then it finds citations and then it scrapes those citations, combines those results.
And it's all represented in code, so we can run it, right?
So then we have a runtime layer.
And so what that does is it allows us to just use all these things for everything, right?
It's not just for delivering services that we're delivering.
Like this one is one where anytime somebody applies to a job, it parses their resume, it gets the job requirements and analyzes their resume.
looks at the answers to the different questions that they answered.
And then it makes a final assessment.
And then for the ones that pass this, we have a recruiter in the loop.
And the thing is, like, you always need some expert or human in the loop for almost everything, right?

And so for us, like, that's what we're trying to do is figure out, like, what are the things that we can use AI in order to replicate what an expert would do, but where an expert is just not worth their time?
Like, is it worth for the CEO of this company to, or the CTO to review every resume applicant?
No.
Then what do people do normally?
They delegate to, like, a lower level person.
But that lower level person is not going to have the same critical thinking as the CEO, for example, right?
So then what do you want to do?
You want to replicate my thought process and then do the last mile with the human in the loop.
And so as we started to do that, we realized this is where the gold is.

The gold is where taking the experts, translating their thought patterns and thought processes into reproducible code that can be ran and learned from, and then reinserting humans and or experts in the loop to guide that process along, but much more efficiently.

And then the process of like where you have the experts and the humans back in the loop is also where it's the byproduct, the messy middle that also you can start to learn from so that with every run and every interaction gets smarter, better, faster.
And so that's kind of like the process we're in right now, right?
Like where essentially like, you know, right now, like it's kind of like our runtime, right?
It's like.
31,000 workflow runs, and then you look at, like, how many activities just this month, 445,000, right?
Well, these are not just any activities.
These are, like, very specific deliverables and steps, right, in those workflows.
So this is, like, all data that we can learn from and use, you know.

So essentially, like, what's happening now, and you know this because you understand this space better, but it's, like, the open AIs of the world are spending billions of dollars paying scale AI and other companies to essentially go hire experts to go do frontier data.
But that frontier data are fake assignments, right?
They're, like, hey, you're, you know, an expert in this topic.
Tell me your thought process on this thing, you know.
And, okay, that may be there, but you know what's really valuable?
Well, you actually delivering an output to a client that's paying you for those services and you collecting all the data and all the interactions that it took, the messy stuff to deliver that output.

And that's real frontier data, you know, that then you can use, you know, and anyways, like, in the TLDR is like, right now, I don't think there's any Canva or any way that is precise and controllable for experts to build the plan to go execute these more complex work, right?

When you look at, like, us delivering one single work output, like an article or something else, like, you're talking 10 plus workflows that have hundreds of steps and process millions of tokens to deliver one thing, you know, like, there's no way an AI model is at a point where they can just, like, guess all those steps to a point.
then from company to company, it's completely different, you know, and so anyways, hopefully that helps to give you context because that's stuff that we don't share publicly too much.
Yeah, no, I appreciate it.
And it resonates, like, even, I'm working on an article now about agents and I have, I'm jumping from model to model, deep research and all the, yeah.
And putting them in separate documents, going back and asking for rewriting in certain styles, and just so I can get something closer to the final product.
But then I still end up, of course, curating myself.
But getting to the point of curation takes me hours.
Exactly, exactly.
But like, so we, I don't know, like, you're someone that has like an amazing, very unique background, like there.
I love to find ways to maybe collaborate, work together.
Like, I don't know, how are you thinking about like, you know, what would be really exciting role potentially for you or a way to collaborate?
Maybe, you know, at least.
So for now, as I told you, I think there's your brother, right?
I said, I'm not looking for a job, but if there are ways to.
I would love to be able to basically build my knowledge and portfolio.
I'm going back to what I said.
It's just about building that muscle because, yes, I can create things on my own, but if they don't solve real problems, they're not as valuable, right?

So if there's any opportunity that I can bring based on what I built on Amazon, which was in a way similar to what you're doing, but instead of creating new workflows, which I think is what you're saying, right, for different use cases, I had created modular architecture that any use case would come in and it would just be a matter of changing a few, like changing the configuration, and then it would be optimized from the application layer down to the actual hardware because cost was important at the time, especially as you're scaling to hundreds of thousands of users, right?
And what I had done was I actually built, we built everything.
From scratch.
don't know if we, I don't think we talked about this.
No, no.
Yeah.
Actually, like, can you tell me?
Yeah, tell me more.
Like, tell me.
Yeah, I'll actually send you the link to the demo.
The demo is much fancier because we cherry picked up outputs.
But we first started with, for Amazon sellers that wanted to promote their products.
And they didn't have graphic designers or they didn't have the funds to hire a marketing agency.
Then we created a one-click solution where they would just select the actual Amazon product from the catalog.
And we would generate the final ad for them.
That's how it started.
And it's very simple, but behind it, you know, we had a workflow of five different models.
And by the time I left, we had 17 that were all orchestrated together.
we were not, it was agendic, as in, you know, depending on the input.
So it's like a mixture of expert type of models.
Models, like smaller models that are like specifics for things or task specific?
we had a mixture of models as one of them, but we would create the actual copy and even generate the prompts that would then generate the images and then consolidate everything.
And then it would be also optimized for placement.
So it's one thing, right, for an ad that goes on your phone versus Prime Video, because we also did them for Prime Video.
And everything was based on Amazon's proprietary data.
So it didn't even need a prompt.
The prompt was optional, but we would pull from the entire product description, the reviews, and also counting seasonality.
And we would generate the optimal advertising for that.
And it did work, right?
We increased engagement.
I don't know the numbers off the top of my head, but I have them.
Yeah.
Um...
Um...
Um...

was also for companies like Coca-Cola or Revlon or the big companies, because it turns out those big companies, they have the budget for their major campaigns.

The Whirlpool, for example, and Duracell.
Yeah.

And we need to go through every single article and find opportunities where we should create illustrations and graphics and make it more visual.
And so we've created these workflows that pull multiple models, multiple APIs, like it uses Recraft for coming up with illustration types.
uses multiple LLMs to come up with illustration concepts.
It parses through and chunks the article and finds which ones are more illustrative in nature that you can illustrate and comes up with different headlines.
It tries to find different layouts.
And, you know, it's just an ensemble of tools.

It's all just like thought processes of like, how would you approach this problem?
For one article.
then how do you make it reproducible for, you know, a thousand articles every month or something, you know, and it's like, it's like, it's the same problem, right?
It's the same, like, approach.
It's a different way to approach it.
But it's like the same, same thing you're describing.
It's awesome.
Yeah.
Yeah.
So we did, and everything was built from scratch, including, I mean, my team fine-tuned, but we also changed model architectures.

And then that expanded to other use cases.
So like for us, like we have an interesting challenge, which
We have all these customers coming to us that either we're already working with or not.
They are all saying, hey, I have this thing.
I don't have engineering capacity to go build custom workflows and build custom solutions, but can you do it for me?
And we've been essentially turning out.
I'll give you an example.
Vista Equity Partners came to us and was like, hey, we have 90 companies that we own.
And they all have the same effing problems on go-to-market, which is like on the sales side, for example, it's like we're standardizing.
That's why we are PE, right?
Like we buy companies and we standardize everything, right?
And we have our paybooks.
We have all our knowledge.
But it's like we need to translate our knowledge into essentially workflows.
So they started looking into tools like NAN and others.
And they're like, I was just going to hire engineers or AI engineers to try to translate our processes into workflows.
But I'm like, this just doesn't work.
Like it just doesn't scale and it's really hard to maintain.
And I was like, then I saw your stuff and I was like, holy .
Yeah, this is it.
Like I bet you guys can do this, you know?
And I was
Yep, that's what we do.

But I'm like, but we're not going to take on this use case right now, because it's like, we're just capacity constraint, not on the tech side, on the layer between, let me understand your problems and translate it into, you know, how we approach it, right?
So we almost have, like, so much, like, potential capacity to go deliver or demand to deliver.
And so we're not demand constraint, and we're not use case constraint.
And we're not, you know, it's the opposite.
It's like, tell me how large of a check you want me to write for you to help me with this thing.
And it's like, no, thank you.
Like, we're in this lane, and this lane is like super repeatable, and we're trying to be thoughtful, right?

So I can see, like, multiple ways that we could think about, like, I mean, someone with your profile, what you've done is like, I think you do really, really well and help supercharge, like, other lanes, and also help run in lanes we currently have.
But
A couple of thoughts here.
one, do you have any restrictions on what you would be able to do if you were still at Microsoft while still working with us or collaborating with us?
I mean, you know, it depends.
If I'm going and blasting everything on LinkedIn about what I'm doing, then that would be a problem, right?
Yeah.
But I'm already engaging, for example, with a healthcare company that I'm doing what I think you're saying, which is translating their use case into actual implementation.
And as long as, you know, I'm in hardware, it doesn't conflict with what you're doing.
But overall, I would just have to be, you know, careful.
Okay.
Okay.
All right.

And the main, the only reason I say that is because like, you know, Microsoft, for example, it's just like Microsoft does everything everywhere, you know, so technically everything comes to Microsoft and the planet, you know, I personally are not like.
It's just mostly like want to be thoughtful.
That's all right.
Like for you.

And I think like potentially if you're open to like, like would be awesome for you to talk to either a CTO or one of our principal engineers just so that they can kind of explain because like right now, pretty much all our principal engineers are forward deploy as part of their onboarding in as well.
What you mean forward deploy?
I don't understand that term.
Yeah.
So for instance, one of our customers is Bolt.U.
They're a competitor of Lovable.
And so we're taking essentially like we're building workflows that take prompt patterns that are prompting Bolt in order to build apps, right?
Like fully functioning apps using Bolt itself.
And then we're creating lending pages.
are kind of like template pages that are starter kits.

You know, so with VAPI, for example, we're doing the same thing, but then all their voice agents are essentially like JSON config files and we're building workflows to essentially pre-build these config files and then embedding these voice agents on pages that describe what those voice agents do.
Like, so AI receptionist for a dentist office, for example, right?

Like, and then so when you search for things like that, like, you know, dentist receptionist or whatever, like you will land on that, but then like have a voice agent that you can use and say, add to my dashboard and build it, right?
Like, so for Webflow, they have like a catalog of made in Webflow pages, 3,000 pages that were enriching.
They also have a bunch of code snippets that people use and they want to build like almost like a snippet like catalogs.

And it's just like, hey, one content type just for my region would save us like two headcounts pretty much because it's like often we have a document that's a thousand pages.
And then we need to customize this document to every single vendor and every single trade for a project.
You know, and then we just send it out and it's just a repetitive process of copy and pasting.
Taking something and then sending to this person, you know, and it's just like two full-time people just for one document type for just this one region.
And it's like, and for one phase of the project, rebuild, you know, and it's like, oh, my God, like, it's just so prone.
And it's like, what tools are you going to use?
They're not AI native.
They're not like they're going to search.
it's like there's nothing out there.
Right.
Take this approach like what Harvey did for legal.
Like there's nobody doing that for construction the same way because, you know.
And so, like, there's all of those that I think could be really, really interesting.
It's just mostly like based on like what's your availability and then us finding like a small enough starting point so that like we can kind of potentially engage.
But there's definitely need there.
That's awesome.
Yeah.
I'd love to chat with your CTO next maybe and to understand and also share my thoughts.

As an architect, but I also have the product muscle, so it probably helps in both ways.

Yeah, exactly.
And what's your level of comfort with reviewing code itself?

tools at the time.

they integrate with Figma.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Explore potential collaboration opportunities between Marcel Santilli (growthx.ai) and Anita Lacea, leveraging Anita's expertise in AI and full-stack knowledge.](https://fathom.video/share/ph6jBoqPRoxumP1Md4CLu9-vZzuSzyRo?tab=summary&timestamp=3.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [growthx.ai has developed a coding agent for creating AI workflows, represented in code, with a runtime layer for execution](https://fathom.video/share/ph6jBoqPRoxumP1Md4CLu9-vZzuSzyRo?tab=summary&timestamp=211.0)
  - [Anita's experience at Amazon includes building modular AI architecture for ad generation, increasing engagement and revenue](https://fathom.video/share/ph6jBoqPRoxumP1Md4CLu9-vZzuSzyRo?tab=summary&timestamp=650.0)
  - [Both parties see potential for collaboration, with growthx.ai having high demand but capacity constraints in certain areas](https://fathom.video/share/ph6jBoqPRoxumP1Md4CLu9-vZzuSzyRo?tab=summary&timestamp=695.0)
  - [Next steps include Anita speaking with growthx.ai's CTO to explore specific collaboration opportunities](https://fathom.video/share/ph6jBoqPRoxumP1Md4CLu9-vZzuSzyRo?tab=summary&timestamp=713.0)

## Topics

### growthx.ai's AI Workflow Platform

  - [Developed a coding agent that writes AI workflows in code](https://fathom.video/share/ph6jBoqPRoxumP1Md4CLu9-vZzuSzyRo?tab=summary&timestamp=378.0)
  - [Runtime layer allows execution of workflows](https://fathom.video/share/ph6jBoqPRoxumP1Md4CLu9-vZzuSzyRo?tab=summary&timestamp=424.0)
  - [Represents complex knowledge work in reproducible code](https://fathom.video/share/ph6jBoqPRoxumP1Md4CLu9-vZzuSzyRo?tab=summary&timestamp=393.0)
  - [Enables experts to build precise, controllable plans for complex work](https://fathom.video/share/ph6jBoqPRoxumP1Md4CLu9-vZzuSzyRo?tab=summary&timestamp=498.0)
  - [Current stats: 31,000 workflow runs, 445,000 activities this month](https://fathom.video/share/ph6jBoqPRoxumP1Md4CLu9-vZzuSzyRo?tab=summary&timestamp=539.0)

### Anita's Experience at Amazon

  - [Built modular AI architecture for ad generation](https://fathom.video/share/ph6jBoqPRoxumP1Md4CLu9-vZzuSzyRo?tab=summary&timestamp=777.0)
  - [System pulled from product descriptions, reviews, and considered seasonality](https://fathom.video/share/ph6jBoqPRoxumP1Md4CLu9-vZzuSzyRo?tab=summary&timestamp=874.0)
  - [Increased engagement and generated $60 million in revenue within a year](https://fathom.video/share/ph6jBoqPRoxumP1Md4CLu9-vZzuSzyRo?tab=summary&timestamp=912.0)
  - [Served both small sellers and large companies like Coca-Cola and Revlon](https://fathom.video/share/ph6jBoqPRoxumP1Md4CLu9-vZzuSzyRo?tab=summary&timestamp=937.0)
  - [Built from scratch, including fine-tuning and modifying model architectures](https://fathom.video/share/ph6jBoqPRoxumP1Md4CLu9-vZzuSzyRo?tab=summary&timestamp=1038.0)

### Potential Collaboration Areas

  - [Translating business problems into AI solutions](https://fathom.video/share/ph6jBoqPRoxumP1Md4CLu9-vZzuSzyRo?tab=summary&timestamp=1567.0)
  - [Architecting and reviewing code for AI workflows](https://fathom.video/share/ph6jBoqPRoxumP1Md4CLu9-vZzuSzyRo?tab=summary&timestamp=1604.0)
  - [Exploring new use cases and industries (e.g., construction, legal)](https://fathom.video/share/ph6jBoqPRoxumP1Md4CLu9-vZzuSzyRo?tab=summary&timestamp=1457.0)
  - [Leveraging Anita's full-stack knowledge and product expertise](https://fathom.video/share/ph6jBoqPRoxumP1Md4CLu9-vZzuSzyRo?tab=summary&timestamp=1560.0)

### Current Market Demand

  - [High demand for AI workflow solutions across various industries](https://fathom.video/share/ph6jBoqPRoxumP1Md4CLu9-vZzuSzyRo?tab=summary&timestamp=1147.0)
  - [Companies seeking help in translating processes into AI workflows](https://fathom.video/share/ph6jBoqPRoxumP1Md4CLu9-vZzuSzyRo?tab=summary&timestamp=1457.0)
  - [growthx.ai currently capacity-constrained, not demand-constrained](https://fathom.video/share/ph6jBoqPRoxumP1Md4CLu9-vZzuSzyRo?tab=summary&timestamp=1230.0)

### Technical Discussion

  - [Use of multiple models and APIs in workflows (e.g., Recraft for illustrations)](https://fathom.video/share/ph6jBoqPRoxumP1Md4CLu9-vZzuSzyRo?tab=summary&timestamp=996.0)
  - [Importance of thought processes in approaching complex problems](https://fathom.video/share/ph6jBoqPRoxumP1Md4CLu9-vZzuSzyRo?tab=summary&timestamp=1015.0)
  - [Discussion of tools like Cursor and Tempo Labs for AI-assisted development](https://fathom.video/share/ph6jBoqPRoxumP1Md4CLu9-vZzuSzyRo?tab=summary&timestamp=1640.0)

## Next Steps

  - [Marcel to introduce Anita to growthx.ai's CTO, Danua](https://fathom.video/share/ph6jBoqPRoxumP1Md4CLu9-vZzuSzyRo?tab=summary&timestamp=1662.0)
  - [Anita and Danua to discuss potential collaboration opportunities in more detail](https://fathom.video/share/ph6jBoqPRoxumP1Md4CLu9-vZzuSzyRo?tab=summary&timestamp=1706.0)
  - [Explore a suitable starting point for engagement, considering Anita's availability and expertise](https://fathom.video/share/ph6jBoqPRoxumP1Md4CLu9-vZzuSzyRo?tab=summary&timestamp=1535.0)
  - [Address any potential conflicts with Anita's current role at Microsoft](https://fathom.video/share/ph6jBoqPRoxumP1Md4CLu9-vZzuSzyRo?tab=summary&timestamp=1273.0)
